column2: ~What services can the general public receive directly from Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The general public can receive the following services directly from Nepal Rastra Bank:

column1: 1. Coin and note exchange
2. Foreign currency exchange
3. Purchase of gold, silver, and gold coins
4. Souvenir coin purchase
5. Purchase and sale of government bonds
6. Hearing and addressing any complaints regarding the activities of banks and financial institutions licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank.
Note: These services are available at both the headquarters of Nepal Rastra Bank and its provincial offices.~

column2: ~What services can the general public receive directly from Nepal Rastra Bank? ~
column3: ~The general public can receive the following services directly from Nepal Rastra Bank:

column1: 1. Coin and note exchange:
2. Foreign currency exchange:
3. Purchase of gold, silver, and gold coins:
4. Souvenir coin purchase:
5. Purchase and sale of government bonds:
6. Hearing and addressing any complaints regarding the activities of banks and financial institutions licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank.
Additionally, citizens can also receive services from the provincial offices of Nepal Rastra Bank, which include:
1. Coin and note exchange:
2. Foreign currency exchange:
3. Purchase of gold, silver, and gold coins:
4. Souvenir coin purchase:
5. Purchase/sale of government bonds:
6. Hearing and addressing any complaints regarding the activities of banks and financial institutions licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~How can one identify whether an organization is licensed/permitted by Nepal Rastra Bank or not?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, an organization licensed/permitted by Nepal Rastra Bank has the words "Licensed/Permitted by Nepal Rastra Bank" written on its signboard. In addition, a list of organizations licensed/permitted by this bank is also kept on the bank's website.~

column2: ~Does Nepal Rastra Bank grant permission to cooperatives?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, Nepal Rastra Bank does not grant permission to cooperatives and does not regulate or supervise such institutions.~

column2: ~How many types of banks and financial institutions are licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~According to the given context, there are 4 types of banks and financial institutions licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank, namely:

column1: 1. Commercial Banks
2. Development Banks
3. Finance Companies
4. Microfinance Financial Institutions~

column2: ~Who can get a loan from Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~According to the context, Nepal Rastra Bank can provide loans and refinances to banks and financial institutions for a maximum period of one year. In addition, the bank can also provide overdraft facilities to the Government of Nepal, if necessary, to repay the loan within one hundred and eighty days.~

column2: ~Who cannot open an account at Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The general public and private organizations cannot open an account at Nepal Rastra Bank. According to the given context, "The Nepal Rastra Bank Act prohibits operating an account for any individual, industry, or political organization." Therefore, only government offices, government organizations/institutions, and banks and financial institutions are eligible to open accounts at Nepal Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~Which entities can open an account at Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the following entities are authorized to open an account at Nepal Rastra Bank:

column1: 1. The Government of Nepal
2. Other government entities
3. Commercial banks and financial institutions
4. Public corporations
5. Foreign missions
6. Foreign central banks
7. International organizations and institutions~

column2: ~What are the main functions of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank mainly performs the following functions:

column1: Issuing Nepalese currency and coins,
Licensing banks and financial institutions and regulating and supervising them,
Formulating monetary policy and foreign exchange policy,
Managing and operating foreign exchange reserves,
Acting as the banker, advisor and financial agent of the Government of Nepal,
Promoting and regulating the payment, settlement and reconciliation system,
and
Managing liquidity.~

column2: ~What is the mission of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The mission of Nepal Rastra Bank is to "achieve macroeconomic and financial stability through effective monetary, foreign exchange, financial sector, and payment system policies for sustainable and inclusive economic development."~

column2: ~What is the vision of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The vision of Nepal Rastra Bank is to be a "modern, dynamic, reliable and effective central bank".~

column2: ~What are the objectives of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The main objectives of Nepal Rastra Bank are to formulate and manage monetary and foreign exchange policies to maintain price and balance of payments stability, to regulate and supervise the banking sector to maintain the stability of the banking and financial sector, and to develop a safe, sound, and efficient payment system.~

column2: ~In which provinces are the offices of Nepal Rastra Bank located?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, Nepal Rastra Bank offices are located in all seven provinces of the country. The specific locations mentioned are:

column1: 1. Biratnagar of Province-1
2. Janakpur and Birgunj of Madhesh Province
3. Pokhara of Gandaki Province
4. Siddharthanagar and Nepalgunj of Lumbini Province
5. Surkhet of Karnali Province
6. Dhangadhi of Sudurpaschim Province
The central office, banking department, and tax division are located in Kathmandu of Bagmati Province.~

column2: ~When was Nepal Rastra Bank established?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank was established on 26 April 1956 (Baisakh 14, 2013).~

column2: ~What kind of bank is Nepal Rastra Bank?, Where is its central office located?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank (NRB) is the Central Bank of Nepal. Its central office is located in Baluwatar, Kathmandu.~

column2: ~What is the role of Nepal Rastra Bank in maintaining price stability?~
column3: ~Maintaining price stability is one of the objectives of Nepal Rastra Bank as mentioned in Section 4 of the Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058. To control demand-side inflation in the economy, Nepal Rastra Bank plays a role in maintaining price stability through appropriate management of monetary variables including interest rates, availability of credit, and money supply through monetary policy.~

column2: ~What is meant by Price Stability?~
column3: ~Price stability means low and stable inflation. It helps maintain the purchasing power of the currency, making it easier for the general public and business people to plan and make decisions related to spending and investment. Maintaining price stability promotes investment and employment in the economy, thereby achieving sustainable economic growth and maintaining macroeconomic stability.~

column2: ~How is consumer inflation measured?~
column3: ~Consumer inflation is measured on the basis of the Consumer Price Index (CPI). The CPI is calculated by collecting the prices of goods and services in the consumer basket determined in the base year 2080/81 on a weekly, monthly, and quarterly basis. The rate of annual point change in the consumer price index thus obtained is called inflation. The current consumer basket includes 525 goods and services. Inflation is measured by collecting prices regularly from 87 market centers in 77 districts across the country.~

column2: ~what items are included in calculating the Consumer Price Index?~
column3: ~The consumer price index (CPI) in Nepal includes 525 goods and services that are used the most by households in Nepal. The selected goods and services have been divided into two main groups: food and beverages, and non-food and services.

column1: Under the food and beverage group, there are 10 subgroups:
1. Pulses and legumes
2. Vegetables
3. Cereal grains and their products
4. Fruits
5. Spices
6. Meat and fish
7. Dairy products
8. Eggs
9. Beverages (soft drinks, tea, coffee)
10. Food items not elsewhere classified
Under the non-food and services group, there are 13 subgroups:
1. Alcoholic beverages
2. Tobacco products
3. Restaurant and hotel meals
4. Clothing and footwear
5. Furnishing and household equipment
6. Health care services
7. Education services
8. Insurance and financial services
9. Transportation services
10. Communication services
11. Recreation and culture services
12. Miscellaneous goods and services
13. Housing (rental, utilities, maintenance)
These items are collected on a regular basis from 87 market centers in 77 districts across the country to calculate the CPI.~

column2: ~How can I get Nepal Rastra Bank publications?~
column3: ~According to the given context, Nepal Rastra Bank publications are mainly available on the bank's website www.nrb.org.np. Some publications are also available in print and such printed publications are also available at the bank's central office, Baluwatar.~

column2: ~What kind of data does Nepal Rastra Bank collect, process and publish?~
column3: ~Nepal Rastra Bank collects, processes, and publishes data on various macroeconomic and financial sector-related issues, including:

column1: 1. Credit, deposit, and financial sector
2. Payment services
3. Prices
4. Financial access
5. Money supply
6. Government revenue and expenditure
7. Balance of payments and international finance
This data is available from various publications and websites of Nepal Rastra Bank under Databases on Nepalese economy.~

column2: ~What is the Salary and Wage Index?~
column3: ~The Salary and Wage Index measures the change in the price of labor (salaries and wages). The base year of this index has been updated to 2080/81. The index has been constructed by collecting data from 1,025 sample organizations in market areas in 30 districts, representing all provinces. This index includes 11 categories under the Nepal Standard Industrial Classification (NSIC) and all categories under the Nepal Standard Classification of Occupations (NSCO).~

column2: ~What is the Wholesale Price Index?~
column3: ~The Wholesale Price Index measures the wholesale price level of goods. It is currently calculated in Nepal by collecting prices on a fortnightly, monthly and quarterly basis, taking the fiscal year 2074/75 as the base year. The index includes two sub-groups under the primary group, two sub-groups under the fuel and energy group, and 13 sub-groups under the production group, with weights of 33.59 percent, 8.76 percent, and 57.65 percent respectively.~

column2: ~Why is there sometimes a discrepancy between the price index (inflation rate) published by the Nepal Rastra Bank and the price increase experienced by the general public?~
column3: ~There may be a discrepancy between the price index (inflation rate) published by the Nepal Rastra Bank and the price increase experienced by the general public because the price index is based on the weighted average price of goods and services included in a specified basket of goods and services. This means that even if the price of a certain good or service increases by a large amount, the average weighted price may only change slightly when the price of another good or service decreases by a large amount. Additionally, the general public may perceive prices differently due to various factors such as their own consumption patterns, geographic location, and purchasing habits, which can lead to differences in experienced inflation rates.~

column2: ~What is consumer inflation?~
column3: ~Consumer inflation refers to the increase in the average price of goods and services consumed by the general consumer. It refers to the rate of change in the average price of goods and services as a whole, rather than the increase in the price of any one good or service.~

column2: ~What is meant by export and import price index?~
column3: ~The Export-Import Price Index measures the unit price of goods exported and imported during a certain period of time. It is also referred to as the terms of trade (ToT). The terms of trade indicate whether the prices of exports have increased more than the prices of imports or vice versa.

column1: If the export-import price index ratio increases, it indicates that the terms of international trade are favorable for the exporting country, meaning that the country can buy more goods and services with its exports due to higher prices received from them. On the other hand, if the export-import price index ratio decreases, it suggests that the terms of international trade are unfavorable for the exporting country.~

column2: ~What is meant by balance of payments position?~
column3: ~The balance of payments position is a summary of the economic and financial transactions between resident and non-resident individuals and institutions within a certain period of time. It represents the total inflows and outflows of foreign currency from an economy, which can result in either a surplus or a deficit. A balance of payments surplus occurs when there are more foreign currency inflows than outflows, while a deficit occurs when there are more outflows than inflows.~

column2: ~What is meant by current account balance?~
column3: ~The current account balance refers to the summary of current economic transactions between resident and non-resident individuals and institutions during a certain period of time. It can be in deficit or surplus. A current account deficit means that net borrowing has been made from other countries, and a current account surplus means that net lending has been made to other countries. The current account consists of the goods and services trade account, the income account, and the transfer account.~

column2: ~What is meant by capital account?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the capital account refers to a part of the balance of payments that deals with transactions related to investments and financing. It includes foreign direct investment, foreign credit inflows, short-term capital inflows, real estate investment, government bonds, portfolio investment in the capital market, and external capital inflows.~

column2: ~What is meant by current account convertibility?~
column3: ~Current account convertibility is the system in which domestic currency can be easily converted into foreign currency and foreign currency into domestic currency for the payment of current transactions, including international trade in goods and services, income, and current transfers.~

column2: ~What is the statutory liquidity ratio?~
column3: ~The statutory liquidity ratio is the obligation that banks and financial institutions must maintain a certain percentage of their total deposits in cash and liquid assets such as government bonds. Currently, commercial banks are required to maintain a statutory liquidity ratio of 12 percent of their total deposit liabilities, including mandatory cash balances, government securities, and cash in the bank's treasury, while development banks and finance companies are required to maintain a statutory liquidity ratio of 10 percent.~

column2: ~What is the required cash reserve ratio?~
column3: ~According to the given context, banks and financial institutions are required to keep four percent of their total deposit liabilities at Nepal Rastra Bank as a mandatory cash reserve.~

column2: ~What is a deposit collection bid?~
column3: ~Deposit collection bid is one of the tools used by Nepal Rastra Bank to absorb excess liquidity in the banking sector for a certain period. Under this, Nepal Rastra Bank collects deposits from banks and financial institutions by determining the interest rate on the basis of a bid, which leads to the absorption of liquidity.~

column2: ~What is a Standing Liquidity Facility?~
column3: ~A Standing Liquidity Facility (SLF) is a credit facility provided by the central bank, Nepal Rastra Bank, to specified institutions against the collateral of eligible securities for short-term liquidity management.~

column2: ~What are the instruments of open market trading?~
column3: ~According to the context, the instruments used in open market trading are:

column1: 1. Repo bids
2. Reverse repo bids
3. Outright purchase bids
4. Outright sale bids
5. Deposit collection bids
6. Nepal Rastra Bank bonds~

column2: ~What is open market operation and how does it work?~
column3: ~Open market operation is the process of buying and selling or absorbing liquidity by the central bank, especially short-term government bonds, in the open market with the aim of managing liquidity in the market and keeping interest rates at a certain level. The central bank buys and sells government bonds in the open market based on the state of inflation and other indicators of the economy.

column1: When the central bank buys bonds in the open market, banks and financial institutions receive cash from the central bank, so the supply of money in the market increases and interest rates decrease. Similarly, when the central bank sells bonds in the open market, the excess liquidity held by banks and financial institutions is absorbed, which reduces the supply of money in the market and may increase interest rates.

In Nepal, the 'Open Market Transactions Operation Committee', formed under the chairmanship of the Deputy Governor of Nepal Rastra Bank, conducts open market transactions. In addition, the Monetary Management Department of the bank also conducts open market transactions.~

column2: ~What is the Liquidity Monitoring and Forecasting Framework (LMFF)?~
column3: ~The Liquidity Monitoring and Forecasting Framework (LMFF) is a framework used by Nepal Rastra Bank to monitor and forecast the liquidity of the banking system based on details such as government financial position, foreign exchange transactions, open market transactions, etc.~

column2: ~What is an interest rate corridor?~
column3: ~The interest rate corridor is a method adopted to keep short-term interest rates, i.e., interbank interest rates, within a certain range to bring stability to long-term interest rates. It also indicates the direction of monetary policy. The rate at which the standing liquidity facility is available acts as an upper limit and the deposit collection rate acts as a lower limit. These rates are changed from time to time by monetary policy as per the need.~

column2: ~What is Base Money/Reserve Money/High Powered Money?~
column3: ~Base money is the sum of money outside banks and financial institutions, money held by banks and financial institutions, balances of banks and financial institutions with the central bank, and other deposits held by the central bank.

column1: Reserve money is defined as the sum of base money and net foreign assets. It increased 0.05 percent in the review period in contrast to a decrease of 7.6 percent in the corresponding period of the previous year. On y-o-y basis, reserve money increased 17.3 percent in mid-September 2024.
High-Powered Money is not explicitly defined in the given context.~

column2: ~What is broad money supply (M2)?~
column3: ~Broad money supply (M2) is the sum of narrow money supply (M1), savings deposits, call deposits, time deposits, and margin deposits. Similarly, broad money supply can also be obtained from the sum of net foreign assets and net domestic assets.~

column2: ~What is narrow money supply (M1)?~
column3: ~Narrow money supply (M1) is the sum of currency in circulation and current deposits.~

column2: ~What are the interim and operational targets of Nepal's monetary policy?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the interim target of Nepal's monetary policy is a stable exchange rate, and the operational target is the interbank interest rate.~

column2: ~What are the main objectives of Nepal's monetary policy?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the main objectives of Nepal's monetary policy are:

column1: To maintain price and balance of payments stability for economic stability and sustainable development of the economy.
This is stated in Article 3 of the Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058.~

column2: ~What is the difference between monetary policy and fiscal policy?~
column3: ~According to the Nepal Rastra Bank Act, 2058, the main objectives of monetary policy are to maintain price and balance of payments stability for economic stability and sustainable development of the economy. The main focus of monetary policy is macroeconomic stability and it works to manage the money supply, credit and interest rates.

column1: On the other hand, fiscal policy refers to the government's policy related to revenue, expenditure and public debt. Fiscal policy is made public in the form of the government's budget.~

column2: ~Who issues monetary policy in Nepal?~
column3: ~The monetary policy is formulated by the Nepal Rastra Bank (NRB), which is the central bank of Nepal.~

column2: ~What is monetary policy?~
column3: ~Monetary policy is the policy formulated by the central bank of a country to support the expansion of economic activity while maintaining macroeconomic stability. It aims to support economic growth and job creation while maintaining economic stability through the management of money supply, credit, and interest rates.~

column2: ~Is it not possible to take a working capital loan in the last month of the quarter (August, December, January, February, March)?~
column3: ~I don’t know based on the given context. The provided text does not mention any restrictions or guidelines related to taking a working capital loan in the last month of the quarter.~

column2: ~Can a term loan of a working capital nature be repaid in advance or not?~
column3: ~Yes, term loans of a working capital nature can be repaid in advance. According to Guidance Point No. 49, "Term loans of a working capital nature can be repaid in advance and there is a provision that banks and financial institutions are not allowed to charge prepayment charges for it."~

column2: ~Does the working capital loan limit apply to all loans?~
column3: ~According to the given context, Answer: No, the working capital loan limit does not apply to loans other than working capital.~

column2: ~Why and under what circumstances does the variance provision apply?~
column3: ~The variance provision applies when the transaction amount in the estimated statement submitted by the borrower while determining the credit limit is more than 20 percent less than the transaction amount in the audit report. This provision aims to make the estimated financial statements submitted by the borrower more realistic.~

column2: ~Can the cash credit limit be used to repay business-related loans?~
column3: ~No, the cash credit limit cannot be used to repay loan-related principal, interest or any other fees.~

column2: ~Who can take an overdraft loan?~
column3: ~According to the given context, only natural persons can take an overdraft loan after the implementation of the Working Capital Loan Guidelines 2079. No organization/firm/company can take such a loan.~

column2: ~Can I take out a term loan for working capital purposes again after taking it once?~
column3: ~According to context 54: "Banks and financial institutions can provide more than one term loan for working capital purposes to the same borrower at different intervals, depending on the borrower's needs, if they see a business need." Therefore, yes, you can take out a term loan for working capital purposes again after taking it once.~

column2: ~Does the 10 percent arrears provision of the procedure apply to all working capital loans?~
column3: ~No, the 10 percent arrears provision of the procedure does not apply to all working capital loans. According to context 55, this provision applies only to cash credit loans, among the four types of working capital loans mentioned in the procedure.~

column2: ~What kind of amount can be deposited in the working capital loan account?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the borrower can deposit money from any source in the cash credit loan account among the various accounts of the working capital loan. However, such deposits should only be made after adequate analysis and cannot be used for any purpose other than commercial purposes. Additionally, transaction/sales proceeds cannot be deposited directly into the working capital loan account; instead, they must be deposited in the borrower's current account, and then transferred to the working capital loan account when necessary.~

column2: ~Can the proceeds from transactions/sales be deposited in the working capital loan account?~
column3: ~No, transaction/sales proceeds cannot be directly deposited into the working capital loan account. Such amounts should be deposited in the borrower's current account first and then transferred to the working capital loan account for repayment of the loan.~

column2: ~Can the working capital loan amount be used for employee expenses?~
column3: ~The borrower can use the working capital loan amount for administrative and operational expenses related to the business. However, such payments must be made in a manner that is directly credited to the bank account.~

column2: ~Can businesses with projects under construction or businesses that have not started sales take working capital loans?~
column3: ~Based on the provided context, it is not explicitly stated whether businesses with projects under construction or businesses that have not started sales can take working capital loans. However, it does mention that "Such businesses are allowed to take working capital loans based on operating expenses and estimated cash and fund flows" in Guidance Point No. 59. This suggests that working capital loans may be available for businesses that are still in the process of starting operations or have projects under construction, but more information would be needed to confirm this specifically.~

column2: ~How many times can an Ad Hoc loan be taken?~
column3: ~According to the given context, an Ad Hoc loan can be taken up to two times in a fiscal year. If more than that is required, the board of directors of the concerned bank and financial institution may decide and provide emergency loans.~

column2: ~Is there a provision to provide other types of working capital loans beyond the above limits?~
column3: ~According to point 67 of the given context, "However, the type of loan provided for working capital purposes should be in accordance with these guidelines." This implies that the guidelines mentioned do not apply to businesses that use working capital loans up to Rs. 10 million (up to a total of Rs. 30 million in the case of manufacturing industries) from the entire banking system. Therefore, it can be inferred that there is no provision for providing other types of working capital loans beyond these limits within these guidelines.~

column2: ~Is there a provision to provide other types of working capital loans beyond the above limits?~
column3: ~According to Guidance Point No. 63: "Working Capital Loan Limit Basis", the working capital loan limit is determined based on the annual estimated turnover and audited financial statements of the company/firm/organization, as well as other indicators (such as Drawing Power) specified by the bank and financial institution in its loan policy.

column1: However, there is an exception mentioned in Guidance Point No. 67: "Working Capital Guideline Exceptions", which states that these guidelines do not apply to businesses that use working capital loans up to Rs. 10 million (up to a total of Rs. 30 million in the case of manufacturing industries) from the entire banking system.
Beyond this limit, it seems that there is no specific provision mentioned in the given context regarding providing other types of working capital loans.~

column2: ~On what basis is the working capital loan limit determined?~
column3: ~The working capital loan limit is determined based on the annual estimated turnover and audited financial statements of the company/firm/organization, as well as other indicators (such as Drawing Power) specified by the bank and financial institution in its loan policy.~

column2: ~For what purposes can a check be issued?~
column3: ~According to the given context, a check can only be issued for business-related payments to be deposited directly into an account. A check cannot be issued to a person, company, or organization with whom there is no business relationship to withdraw or transfer funds.~

column2: ~Can a check be issued for a working capital loan?~
column3: ~According to point 65. Cash Credit Check Issuance: Among working capital loans, only a check can be issued for a cash credit loan. However, a check cannot be issued to a person, company, or organization with whom there is no business relationship to withdraw or transfer funds.~

column2: ~For what period is a working capital loan provided?~
column3: ~Working capital loans are generally provided for a period of less than one year. However, for Permanent Working Capital Needs, a term loan of at least three years and a maximum of ten years is provided.~

column2: ~Are the guidelines for working capital loans applicable to all businesses?~
column3: ~According to context 68: "The loan provided by banks and financial institutions to manage the gap between the current assets and current liabilities of any organization/firm/company is called a working capital loan. Only organizations/firms/companies are eligible to take this type of loan. No natural person can take this type of loan."

column1: So, the guidelines for working capital loans are applicable only to businesses (organizations/firms/companies), not to individuals or natural persons.~

column2: ~What is a working capital loan and who can take it?~
column3: ~A working capital loan is a type of loan provided by banks and financial institutions to manage the gap between the current assets and current liabilities of any organization/firm/company. Only organizations/firms/companies are eligible to take this type of loan. No natural person can take this type of loan.~

column2: ~What types of loans are included in the “loans related to the construction sector” mentioned in the provisions related to rescheduling and/or restructuring ?~
column3: ~According to the context, "loans directly related to construction, including cement, rods, bricks/blocks, pipes and fittings" are included in the loans related to the construction sector. Additionally, it is also mentioned that these loans are mentioned in the integrated directive issued by Nepal Rastra Bank to “A”, “B” and “C” class licensed institutions, Serial No. 6 of N.R. Bank Directive F.No.9.3 of 2079.~

column2: ~How is the interest rate determined?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, Nepal Rastra Bank has issued a unified directive to licensed institutions of “A”, “B” and “C” categories. While determining and publishing the interest rate to be provided on deposits, licensed banks and financial institutions may determine the interest rate by changing the average of the maximum interest rate (except for the accounts specified in sub-point 5 and sub-point 7) and the minimum interest rate (except for call deposits) published by all institutions of the concerned category in the previous month by a maximum of ten percent. If the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates established while determining the interest rate exceeds the limit of the provision of sub-point 5, then one of the maximum or minimum rates should be maintained and the other rates should be adjusted.~

column2: ~What is the interest rate of which deposit?~
column3: ~The context does not provide specific information about the interest rate of a particular deposit. It provides general guidelines for determining deposit interest rates, but does not mention a specific deposit or its interest rate.~

column2: ~If the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates remains 5 percent when the interest rate is increased, is it necessary to adjust the rates?~
column3: ~According to the context, if the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates remains within the limit of 5 percent, no adjustment can be made. In such a case, the interest rates of all deposits should be adjusted and kept within this limit. No further adjustment is necessary.~

column2: ~If the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates increases and the interest rate exceeds the limit of five percent, is it necessary to adjust the rates?~
column3: ~Yes, if the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates increases and the interest rate exceeds the limit of five percent, the rates must be adjusted compulsorily.~

column2: ~If the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates increases beyond the limit of five percent, how can the rates be adjusted?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, if the difference between the maximum and minimum interest rates exceeds the limit of 5 percent, the rates must be adjusted compulsorily. In this situation, the licensed institution should take either the maximum or minimum interest rate as the basis for adjustment. If the maximum interest rate is 9.35 percent, then to maintain a 5 percent difference between the interest rates, the minimum interest rate of that bank should be kept at a maximum of 4.35 percent and other rates should be adjusted accordingly. Similarly, if the minimum interest rate is 3.85 percent, then the maximum interest rate of that bank should be determined at 8.85 percent and other rates should be adjusted to maintain a 5 percent difference between the interest rates.~

column2: ~Should the maximum and minimum interest rates be based on separate bases for each type of deposit?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, there is no need to base the maximum and minimum interest rates on each type of deposit. It states: "There is no need to base the maximum and minimum interest rates on each deposit."~

column2: ~Is it possible to pledge shares as collateral for doing business?~
column3: ~Yes, entrepreneurs and businessmen can take loans by pledging shares along with other collateral for their industrial and business activities. Additionally, banks and financial institutions can also pledge shares as collateral as additional collateral for loans disbursed for business purposes, just like they pledge other assets.~

column2: ~At what interest rate is a loan available for micro, household, small and medium businesses?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, there is no specific information on the interest rate for loans available for micro, household, small, and medium-sized businesses. However, it is mentioned that in the case of starting an agricultural, handicraft, and skill-based business or other enterprise business with a loan amount up to Rs. 10 million, a fixed interest rate can be taken by adding a premium of up to 2 percent to the base rate of banks and financial institutions for businesses other than import-related businesses.~

column2: ~Can microfinance institutions collect deposits from the general public?~
column3: ~No, microfinance institutions in Nepal are not allowed to collect savings deposits from individuals other than their members. Only limited branches of Nirdhan Utthan Microfinance Institution Limited and Chhimek Microfinance Institution Limited have been granted permission to collect deposits from the general public.~

column2: ~How much interest rate and service fee does a microfinance financial institution charge on a loan?~
column3: ~According to the context, a microfinance financial institution can charge an interest rate of up to 15 percent and a service fee of up to 1.50 percent as specified in the circular on interest rate and service fee issued by Nepal Rastra Bank from time to time.~

column2: ~What is the limit of loans disbursed by microfinance financial institutions?~
column3: ~According to the context, microfinance financial institutions can disburse unsecured microloans up to Rs. 300,000 per member (maximum Rs. 500,000 for members who have availed loans for the past 2 years and are in good standing), collateralized loans of up to Rs. 700,000 for poor and low-income individuals, firms or groups engaged in specified agriculture, microenterprises and businesses, and up to Rs. 1.5 million for individuals who have availed loans for the past 2 years and are in good standing.~

column2: ~Who regulates and supervises microfinance institutions?~
column3: ~Microfinance financial institutions are regulated by Nepal Rastra Bank, Department of Banking and Financial Institutions Regulation and supervised by Nepal Rastra Bank, Department of Microfinance Institutions Supervision.~

column2: ~What is meant by a microfinance financial institution?~
column3: ~An institution that obtains a license from Nepal Rastra Bank, makes poor and low-income individuals, especially women, members, collects regular savings from them, and provides microfinance loans without collateral to operate agricultural and microenterprise businesses on a collective basis, and provides collateral loans up to a certain limit to operate microenterprises and microbusinesses by taking acceptable collateral from its members or other persons, and collects savings from members in limited amounts is called a microfinance financial institution.~

column2: ~Where should I contact to obtain or provide information related to a bank or financial institution?~
column3: ~According to the given context, banks and financial institutions must mandatorily obtain credit information of customers from the Credit Information Center before disbursing credit. Similarly, customers can file complaints on disputed payments, transactions, and other issues in electronic banking delivery channels, which will be handled by the bank's grievance handling mechanism.~

column2: ~How does the Credit Information Center prepare credit information details?~
column3: ~Based on the given context, it is mentioned that "Credit Information Preparation: is a provision that any bank or financial institution must inform the Credit Information Center within the specified period of time about all the loans/facilities approved by it, details of all loans/facilities that are overdue by more than 90 days, and loan amounts of one million rupees or more to be blacklisted."~

column2: ~Under what circumstances is credit information obtained?~
column3: ~According to the given context, credit information is obtained under the following circumstance:

column1: 85. Credit Information Rules: Any bank and financial institution must mandatorily obtain the credit information of that customer from the Credit Information Center before disbursing credit to any of its customers, renewing old loans, restructuring or rescheduling loans.~

column2: ~What is a loan information?~
column3: ~According to the given context, loan information is defined as "the information that banks and financial institutions obtain from the Credit Information Center established under the Companies Act regarding the loans taken by any of their customers (individuals, firms, companies or organizations) from various banks and financial institutions and their status."~

column2: ~What is meant by financial interest?~
column3: ~According to context, if a director of a bank or financial institution, a shareholder or chief executive officer holding one percent or more of the shares, or a family member of such a person or a person with the authority to nominate a director, company or corporate body purchases shares, individually or jointly, amounting to ten percent or more of the paid-up capital of any firm, company or corporate body, such person or persons shall have an interest in the institution in which they own ten percent or more of the shares.~

column2: ~What is an Early Warning Signal?~
column3: ~An early warning signal is a situation where the bank or financial institution concerned may face a potentially bad situation in the near future, based on the analysis and monitoring of changes in any index related to the bank or financial institution, important information received, the financial position of the bank concerned, the status of compliance with instructions, etc.~

column2: ~What is stress testing?~
column3: ~Stress testing is the study of the pressure or change in the financial indicators of a bank or financial institution when imagining an economic and financial situation different from the current one. Stress testing is carried out to study the impact that fluctuations in the economy due to internal and external factors may have on the capital, liquidity, and income of any bank or financial institution.~

column2: ~What is the Compulsory Cash Reserve Ratio (CRR)?~
column3: ~There is a provision that banks and financial institutions must mandatorily deposit a certain percentage of their total deposit liabilities in cash in an account opened with Nepal Rastra Bank. Such a balance is called the compulsory cash balance, while the fixed ratio of the deposit liabilities specified to determine the amount to be kept in the account with the Rastra Bank as compulsory balance is called the compulsory cash reserve ratio (CRR).~

column2: ~What is an off-balance sheet transaction?~
column3: ~Off-balance-sheet transactions are transactions that may create liabilities for a bank or financial institution, such as letters of credit, guarantees, acceptances, commitments, foreign exchange swaps, options, and forward transactions, and the term also refers to transactions in instruments specified by the Nepal Rastra Bank from time to time.~

column2: ~What are Risk Weighted Assets?~
column3: ~Risk-weighted assets are the total number obtained by multiplying the amount of each item on and off the balance sheet of a bank or financial institution by the specified risk weight.~

column2: ~What is CAMELS rating?~
column3: ~CAMELS rating is a method of evaluating the capital adequacy (C), asset quality (A), management quality (M), return position (E), liquidity position (L), and market risk sensitivity (S) of the concerned bank or financial institution as a conclusion of the inspection work after the completion of the on-site inspection.~

column2: ~What are the provisions regarding interest and penalties for the overnight liquidity facility?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, if the daily liquidity facility taken by banks and financial institutions is not repaid on the same working day, the remaining amount will be converted into an overnight liquidity facility and interest will be calculated on that facility at the bank rate. Additionally, if the institution needs additional facilities without repaying the remaining amount of the overnight liquidity facility, an additional one percent will be added to the overnight liquidity facility of the previous working day and interest will be calculated on the overnight liquidity facility of that working day and the facility will be provided by calculating the interest at the bank rate.~

column2: ~What are the provisions regarding the daily liquidity facility and overnight liquidity facility limits?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the provisions regarding the daily liquidity facility and overnight liquidity facility limits are as follows:

column1: 1. Daily Liquidity Facility Limits: The securities as per Regulation 26 of the Nepal Rastra Bank Open Market Transactions Regulations (First Amendment, 2079), 2078 will be kept as collateral in the Monetary Management Department of this bank and the daily liquidity facility limit will be set up to a maximum of 90 percent. However, the amount up to the daily liquidity facility limit can be used for overnight liquidity facility.

2. Overnight Liquidity Facility (OLF): If an institution has insufficient funds to return the borrowed funds to the Collateral Account before the end of business day, the outstanding amount will be converted into OLF against the same collateral as long as it has not expired.~

column2: ~What is meant by Overnight Liquidity Facility?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, Overnight Liquidity Facility (OLF) refers to a facility provided by the Nepal Rastra Bank for the overnight period at a specified interest rate, for the amount remaining to be cleared within the specified time limit of the same day out of the daily liquidity facility utilized by an institution.~

column2: ~What is meant by Intraday Liquidity Facility?~
column3: ~Intraday liquidity facility (ILF) refers to a credit line provided by the Service Provider to Participant banks during business hours to ensure sufficient liquidity for settlement purposes in the Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) system. It allows participant banks to borrow funds against eligible assets registered in their Collateral Account, which can be used to meet temporary shortfalls and maintain efficiency in the financial system.~

column2: ~What precautions should be taken while making electronic payment transactions?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, some precautions that should be taken while making electronic payment transactions are:

column1: 1. Users should not provide their secret password, One Time Password (OTP) or sensitive details (such as account number, date of birth, citizenship or passport number, etc.) to other people by any means.
2. They should change their passwords from time to time and keep such passwords confidential.
3. If the device used for payment such as card, mobile phone, etc. is stolen or lost, the service should be blocked by informing the concerned service provider immediately.
Additionally, it can also be inferred that users should:
* Be aware of the risks and benefits of using e-banking delivery services
* Educate themselves on which protections are provided and not provided in each of their delivery channels
* Ensure the security of information stored in mobile devices
* Encrypt transaction information and PIN/Password from mobile devices to bank's system while providing banking services using mobile devices~

column2: ~How much money can be transferred through the electronic wallet?~
column3: ~According to the given context, up to Rs. 50,000 per transaction, up to Rs. 100,000 per day, and up to Rs. 100,000 per month can be transferred through the electronic wallet.~

column2: ~What is a Quick Response (QR) Code?~
column3: ~A special type of Bar Code that can be scanned by an electronic payment device to retrieve the details stored in it. It contains the account details of the merchant receiving the payment. Currently, as per the instructions of this bank, there is no charge to the customer for making payments through QR Code.~

column2: ~What is e-money?~
column3: ~E-money, also known as Virtual Money, is the amount deposited in an electronic wallet, prepaid card or other electronic device that allows physical cash held in banks and financial institutions to be transacted electronically.~

column2: ~What is an NFC (Near Field Communication) card?~
column3: ~An NFC (Near Field Communication) card is a card used to make payments for small amounts without entering a PIN code or OTP code.~

column2: ~What is Point of Sale (POS)?~
column3: ~Point of Sale (PoS) is an electronic payment acceptance device that allows various merchants or shops to accept payment for goods and services purchased or consumed by customers through various cards currently available.~

column2: ~What is Issuer Bank and Acquirer Bank?~
column3: ~According to the given context, an Issuer Bank is a bank that issues any electronic payment instrument such as a payment card or QR. An Acquirer Bank is a bank with a terminal where the electronic payment instrument issued by the Issuer Bank is used for transactions.~

column2: ~What is meant by On-Us and Off-Us transactions in ATM transactions?~
column3: ~According to the given context, On-Us transactions are defined as "transactions made using a payment card issued by any bank and financial institution at the ATM terminal of the same institution."

column1: On the other hand, Off-Us transactions are defined as "transactions made using a payment card issued by one bank and financial institution at the ATM terminal of another bank and financial institution."~

column2: ~How much payment transaction can be made through electronic card?~
column3: ~The maximum cash withdrawal limit using electronic cards is as follows:

column1: * Banks and financial institutions – Prepaid cards: up to Rs. 10,000 per transaction, up to Rs. 20,000 per day, and up to Rs. 2 lakh per month.
* Banks and financial institutions – Debit Card: up to Rs. 25,000 per transaction, up to Rs. 1 lakh per day, and up to Rs. 4 lakh per month.
* Banks and financial institutions – Credit Card: Up to 10 percent of the approved limit.
Please note that these limits may vary depending on the institution's specific policies and instructions.~

column2: ~What are the provisions regarding fees charged for payment transactions?~
column3: ~According to the context, the provisions regarding fees charged for payment transactions are as follows:

column1: 107. Payment Transaction Fees: As per the instructions of this bank, the licensed institution has determined the fees charged according to the nature of the payment transaction. The updated details regarding the fees so determined are published on the website of the concerned institution.

131. Fees for Payment Transactions: Each institution determines the fees charged according to the nature of the payment transaction. Updated details regarding the fees determined in this way are published on the website of the concerned institution.

Additionally, there is a mention of ATM Withdrawal Fee for Other Banks (130) which states that when a payment card issued by one bank and financial institution is used at an ATM of another bank and financial institution within the country, a fee of up to Rs.15 may be charged.~

column2: ~Do banks and financial institutions licensed by this bank need to obtain permission to perform payment-related activities?~
column3: ~Yes, banks and financial institutions licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank need to obtain permission to perform payment-related activities. As per instruction 108 of the provided context, "Banks and financial institutions licensed by this bank need to obtain permission to perform payment-related activities."~

column2: ~Which organizations can perform electronic payment-related activities?~
column3: ~According to the given context, apart from banks and financial institutions, a total of 37 institutions, including 10 payment system operators and 27 payment service providers, are currently operating payment-related activities after obtaining licenses from Nepal Rastra Bank. Banks and financial institutions licensed by this bank can also perform payment-related activities after obtaining licenses.~

column2: ~Which institutions can currently conduct RTGS transactions?~
column3: ~According to the context, RTGS transactions can be conducted from any branch of the banks and financial institutions affiliated with this system. The list of banks and financial institutions that can conduct RTGS transactions can be viewed on the bank's website https://www.nrb.org.np/departments/psd/.~

column2: ~How much transaction amount can be paid through the RTGS system?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, any amount of Rs. 2 lakh or more can be paid through the RTGS system in case of Nepali Rupee and Japanese Yen, while for US Dollar, Euro, and British Pound, any amount of Rs. 200,000 or more can be transacted through this system.~

column2: ~What is the difference between RTGS and other electronic payment instruments?~
column3: ~Transactions made through RTGS are credited to the customer's account immediately, while transactions made through other large-value payment instruments (such as ECC, IPS) are not credited to the customer's account immediately but take some time. The key difference between RTGS and other electronic payment instruments lies in the nature of settlement and the type of transactions they serve.~

column2: ~How can I get more information about RTGS?~
column3: ~You can obtain more information about the Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) system by contacting Nepal Rastra Bank, Payment Systems Department, RTGS Operation Center at 01-4428202 or referring to the RTGS System Rules, 2019.~

column2: ~How can financial institutions that do not participate in the RTGS system transact?~
column3: ~Financial institutions that do not participate in the RTGS system can transact as indirect members through participating banks that are direct members of this system.~

column2: ~What are the benefits of transacting through the RTGS system?~
column3: ~Transacting through the RTGS system reduces settlement risk, is safe and reliable as transaction details are immediately visible in the RTGS system, and accelerates economic activity as payment for transactions is made immediately.~

column2: ~What is the operating period of the RTGS system?~
column3: ~There is a provision for customer transactions (Customer Transfer) and interbank transactions (Interbank Payments) to be done in the Morning Exchange for Customer and Interbank Payments and Afternoon Exchange for Customer and Interbank Payments Period, while the Evening Exchange for Interbank Payments Period is reserved for interbank RTGS transactions only.~

column2: ~Which currencies and up to what amount can be transacted through the RTGS system?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the following currencies can be transacted through the RTGS system:

column1: * Nepali Rupee (NPR)
* US Dollar (USD)
* Euro (EURO)
* British Pound (GBP)
* Japanese Yen (JPY)
The transaction amount limit for RTGS is Rs. 2 lakh or more.~

column2: ~How long does it take for a transaction to be completed through the RTGS system?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, transactions through the RTGS system are usually completed within a few minutes.~

column2: ~How much is the fee for trading through the RTGS system?~
column3: ~According to the context, the fee for trading through the RTGS system varies depending on the period of transaction. The fees are as follows:

column1: * Rs. 10 for trading during the Morning Exchange for Customer and Interbank Payments Period
* Rs. 20 for trading during the Afternoon Exchange for Customer and Interbank Payments Period
* Rs. 100 for trading during the Evening Exchange for Interbank Payments Period~

column2: ~Which institutions can currently conduct RTGS transactions?~
column3: ~According to the given context, RTGS transactions can be conducted from any branch of banks and financial institutions affiliated with this system. The list of banks and financial institutions that can conduct RTGS transactions can be viewed on the bank's website. Additionally, it is mentioned that financial institutions that do not participate in the RTGS system can transact as indirect members through participating banks that are direct members of this system.~

column2: ~What is the RTGS system?~
column3: ~The Real-Time Gross Settlement (RTGS) System is an electronic payment system used for large value and urgent payments. It mainly handles transactions of Rs. 2 lakh or more, which are credited to the customer's account immediately. The key difference between RTGS and other electronic payment instruments lies in the nature of settlement and the type of transactions they serve.~

column2: ~How much money can be transferred through an electronic wallet?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, up to Rs. 50,000 per transaction, up to Rs. 100,000 per day, and up to Rs. 100,000 per month can be transferred through the wallet. The bank may change the limit for such transfer by issuing instructions from time to time.~

column2: ~How much can be paid through mobile and internet banking?.~
column3: ~According to the given context, up to Rs. 3 lakh can be paid per day through mobile banking, and up to Rs. 2 lakh can be paid per day through internet banking.~

column2: ~Does the amount deposited in the mobile wallet opened by customers earn interest?~
column3: ~According to the given context, "Generally, the amount deposited in the mobile wallet opened by customers does not earn interest."~

column2: ~What is a debit card?~
column3: ~A debit card is a card issued in the name of a customer to allow withdrawals and payments from a bank account.~

column2: ~What is a credit card?~
column3: ~A credit card is a card that allows the customer to make payments even if they do not have money in their bank account. The cardholder must repay the amount spent on such a card to the relevant bank or financial institution after a certain period of time, along with a specified fee.~

column2: ~How many types of payment cards are prevalent in Nepal?~
column3: ~According to the given context, there are currently three types of payment cards prevalent in Nepal: credit card, debit card, and prepaid card.~

column2: ~How much is the fee for withdrawing money from an ATM of another bank using an ATM card of one bank?~
column3: ~According to the given context, a fee of up to Rs.15 may be charged when a payment card issued by one bank and financial institution is used at an ATM of another bank and financial institution within the country.~

column2: ~What are the provisions regarding fees charged for payment transactions?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the provisions regarding fees charged for payment transactions are as follows:

column1: * As per the instructions of this bank, the licensed institution has determined the fees charged according to the nature of the payment transaction. (107. Payment Transaction Fees)
* The updated details regarding the fees so determined are published on the website of the concerned institution.

* Each institution determines the fees charged according to the nature of the payment transaction. Updated details regarding the fees determined in this way are published on the website of the concerned institution. (131. Fees for Payment Transactions)~

column2: ~Which are the payment institutions currently operating?, Can banks and financial institutions perform payment-related activities?~
column3: ~According to the given context, apart from banks and financial institutions, a total of 37 institutions, including 10 payment system operators and 27 payment service providers, are currently operating payment-related activities after obtaining licenses from Nepal Rastra Bank.

column1: Yes, banks and financial institutions licensed by this bank can perform payment-related activities after obtaining licenses.~

column2: ~What do payment service providers do?~
column3: ~Payment service providers provide direct services to customers in electronic payment transactions. These organizations conduct electronic payment transactions between customers and service providers, such as making payments for goods, services, assets, or other liabilities, and transferring funds domestically and internationally.~

column2: ~How can revenue be paid to Nepal from abroad?~
column3: ~According to the given context, revenue can be paid to Nepal from abroad by sending the amount to be deposited in a US dollar account maintained by the Office of the Comptroller and Auditor General at the Nepal Rastra Bank's Nostro Account located abroad.~

column2: ~Can/Cannot exchange transactions be made through QR Code?~
column3: ~According to the context, transactions can be made through QR Code for foreign exchange transactions. As stated in point 136: "When exchanging Indian rupees and other foreign currencies, Nepali rupees can be paid in cash and through QR Code."~

column2: ~In cases where the Government of Nepal has declared an emergency public holiday and decided to operate banking services as essential services on such holidays, which banking services will be provided on that day?~
column3: ~According to the context, in cases where the Government of Nepal has declared an emergency public holiday and decided to operate banking services as essential services on such holidays, the following banking services can be provided:

column1: * Cash payments
* ECC/IPS (Electronic Clearing and Collection/Payment System)
* Public debt management
* RTGS (Real-Time Gross Settlement)~

column2: ~What is the maximum amount of cheque payment that can be made through ECC System?~
column3: ~According to the given context, a maximum amount of N.R. 30 crore can be paid through cheque through ECC System.~

column2: ~Is it possible to deposit money into a personal account of a person in Nepal via SWIFT from abroad or not?~
column3: ~According to the context, it is not possible to send money to an account in Nepal Rastra Bank to deposit money into an individual's account. However, you can send money to the SWIFT code of the bank or financial institution in the name of the person in whose name the money is to be deposited, so that the money is deposited into that person's account (Ref: 144).~

column2: ~What are the trading hours of Electronic Cheque Clearing System (ECC) / Interbank Payment System (IPS) in the banking department?  ~
column3: ~According to the given context, the trading hours of Electronic Cheque Clearing System (ECC) and Interbank Payment System (IPS) in the Banking Department are:

column1: * ECC: Sunday – Thursday from 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM and Friday from 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM
* IPS: Sunday – Thursday from 10:00 AM to 5:00 PM, Friday from 10:00 AM to 1:00 PM~

column2: ~How many types of sessions are there in ECC transactions conducted by the Banking Department?~
column3: ~According to the given context, there are three types of ECC transaction sessions:

column1: 1. Regular sessions: Conducted from Monday to Thursday from 10:00 am to 3:00 pm and on Friday from 10:00 am to 12:00 pm.
2. Express sessions: Three express sessions of 1 hour each are conducted every day from 10:00 am to 1:00 pm.
3. High Value sessions: Two high-value sessions are conducted from 10:00 am to 3:00 pm.~

column2: ~Can a person open an account at Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~No, a person cannot open a personal account at Nepal Rastra Bank. According to information 142 and 8 in the given context, accounts can only be opened by government offices, government organizations/institutions, commercial banks and financial institutions, public corporations, foreign missions, foreign central banks, foreign banks, and international organizations and institutions at Nepal Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~What are the scheduled hours for banking transactions conducted by the Banking Department?~
column3: ~The scheduled hours for banking transactions conducted by the Banking Department are from 10:00 AM to 4:00 PM on Sundays to Thursdays and from 10:00 AM to 12:30 PM on Fridays. However, there is an exception during Kartik 16 to Magh 15 when the office is open from 10:00 AM to 3:00 PM.~

column2: ~When sending money from abroad to an account in a bank or financial institution in Nepal via SWIFT, which account in Nepal Rastra Bank should be sent?~
column3: ~According to the given context, it is not possible to send money to an account in Nepal Rastra Bank. The amount can only be sent to the SWIFT code of a bank or financial institution in the name of the person in whose name the money is to be deposited, so that the money is deposited into that person's account.~

column2: ~What arrangements have been made for government banking transactions in Nepal?~
column3: ~Government banking arrangements in Nepal are conducted through the Banking Department of Nepal Rastra Bank, provincial offices, and branches and extension counters of commercial banks approved to conduct government transactions.~

column2: ~What should be done to deposit a deposit or other amount in the account of the Government of Nepal?~
column3: ~To deposit a deposit or other amount in the account of the Government of Nepal, you need to fill in all the details mentioned in the specified voucher, including the office code of that office. When depositing a deposit through check clearing, in addition to the above-mentioned details, the name of the office to which the amount is to be sent for deposit must be mentioned and the office operating the account must have stamped and stamped the back of the check.~

column2: ~How can the revenue to be paid to the Government of Nepal be paid through the banking system?~
column3: ~The revenue to be paid to the Government of Nepal can be paid through the banking system in the following ways:

column1: 1. By depositing a deposit or other amount in the account of the Government of Nepal at the Banking Department of Nepal Rastra Bank and provincial offices.
2. By paying revenue through check clearing, mentioning the name of the office to which the amount is to be sent for deposit, and having the check stamped by the office operating the account.
3. By logging into Connect IPS operated by Nepal Clearing House Limited (NCHL) and rajaswa.fcgo.gov.np operated by the Office of the Comptroller General for some offices.
Additionally, permission has been given to commercial banks concerned for revenue collection work, which can be done only from the bank branch/counter designated for the purpose.~

column2: ~How can foreign currency be exchanged from the banking department?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, Nepali citizens traveling to countries other than India can exchange foreign currency up to a maximum of US$2,500.- or equivalent convertible foreign currency from the banking department based on the required documents and other necessary documents specified by this bank, depending on the cash balance. However, such exchange facility will be provided only not more than twice in a year.~

column2: ~How much Indian Rupees can be exchanged while traveling to India for medical treatment?~
column3: ~According to the context, in case the patient himself or his relatives are unable to attend the trip to India for medical treatment along with the original document recommended by a registered doctor recognized in Nepal, the exchange facility can be provided to the patient up to a maximum of INR 25,000.- depending on the INR cash balance in this bank.~

column2: ~How much Indian rupees can be exchanged when traveling to India for study, pilgrimage, and other purposes?~
column3: ~According to the given context, a maximum of INR 10,000 per person can be provided with exchange facilities when traveling to India for study, pilgrimage, and other purposes.~

column2: ~What are the arrangements made by Nepal Rastra Bank regarding Indian Rupee exchange for Indian citizens?~
column3: ~According to the context, Nepal Rastra Bank provides Indian Rupee exchange to Indian citizens. Indian citizens who are in Nepal for various professional and other reasons can be provided with exchange facility up to a maximum of Indian Rupees 5,000 once a week, based on original documents proving their identity, need, and justification.~

column2: ~What are the arrangements made by Nepal Rastra Bank regarding the exchange of Indian Rupees for Nepali citizens?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, Nepal Rastra Bank makes the following arrangements regarding the exchange of Indian Rupees for Nepali citizens:

column1: * A maximum of INR 10,000 can be exchanged in a fortnight based on the original documents of citizenship or other identity cards issued by the Government of Nepal.
Note: This information is mentioned in paragraph 152 of the provided context.~

column2: ~What facilities can the general public get from the Banking Department of Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~The general public can receive services related to coin and note exchange, foreign currency exchange, purchase of gold, silver and gold coins, souvenir coins, purchase/sale of government bonds, and hearing and addressing any complaints regarding the activities of banks and financial institutions licensed by Nepal Rastra Bank from the Banking Department of Nepal Rastra Bank.~

column2: ~What is the weight of the gold bars that the general public can buy and what is their price?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the gold bars that the general public can buy from the Exchequer are 2.5 grams, 5 grams, and 10 grams. Their prices are determined daily in line with the fluctuations in market prices.~

column2: ~Can gold and silver bullion/coins purchased from the mint be resold in the same branch?~
column3: ~No, according to context 155, "Gold and silver coins purchased from the mint cannot be resold in the same branch. That is, Nepal Rastra Bank sells gold and silver coins to the public, not purchases them from the general public."~

column2: ~Where can I buy souvenir coins and medallions sold by Nepal Rastra Bank?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, you can purchase souvenir coins and medallions from the Nepal Rastra Bank, Currency Management Department, Minting Division, Babarmahal.~

column2: ~From which offices can gold and silver bullion/coins sold by Nepal Rastra Bank be purchased?~
column3: ~Gold and silver bullion/coins sold by Nepal Rastra Bank can be purchased from Nepal Rastra Bank, the Mint Division under the Currency Management Department, Babarmahal and provincial offices.~

column2: ~On which days can the general public purchase coins from the Nepal Rastra Bank's Mint Division?~
column3: ~The general public can purchase coins from the Nepal Rastra Bank's Mint Division on Sundays to Thursday from 10 am to 4 pm (from Kartik 16 to Magh 15 from 10 am to 3 pm) and on Friday from 10 am to 12 pm.~

column2: ~Where is the Nepal Rastra Bank's Currency Division located?~
column3: ~The Currency Division of Nepal Rastra Bank is currently located in the Nepal Aushadhi Limited building in Babarmahal, Kathmandu.~

column2: ~How is Nepali currency printed?~
column3: ~The printing of Nepali currency is done by selecting a security printing company through international bidding.~

column2: ~How can banknotes be protected?~
column3: ~Banknotes can be protected by keeping them in a wallet or purse without folding them and not applying any type of paint. Do not write on the banknotes carelessly. Similarly, do not damage the banknotes by cutting, tearing, stitching, or gluing them, which can shorten their lifespan.~

column2: ~What is a note fund?~
column3: ~According to the given context, "Note Fund" means a fund set up by Nepal Rastra Bank for the smooth supply of Nepali rupees throughout Nepal. Currently, there are a total of 79 note funds in the Kingdom of Nepal, including 8 of Nepal Rastra Bank, 45 of Rastriya Banijya Bank, and 26 of Nepal Bank Limited. Such note funds are generally located in government banks located in district headquarters.~

column2: ~What happens if you arbitrarily write or draw on a note or deface it by cutting it?~
column3: ~According to the context, notes with arbitrary writing, cut or defaced notes will not be exchanged. The bank will refuse to exchange such notes and confiscate them, providing information along with the reason to the person concerned.~

column2: ~How can a fake note be identified?~
column3: ~A fake note can be identified by recognizing some of the features that can be seen with the naked eye. For example, genuine notes have a watermark, which when held up to light, shows the shape of a lily flower. Such watermark is not clearly visible on fake notes. Genuine notes have a security thread, which when held up to light, has English words written on it and does not cover any letters. Such thread on fake notes may cover the letters. The paper used in genuine notes is somewhat rough and the print quality is also good. The paper of fake notes is slippery and the print quality is not good. The color of genuine notes does not fade or fades even when soaked in water, whereas the color of fake notes fades and fades when soaked in water.~

column2: ~What should you do if you suspect that a note is counterfeit?~
column3: ~If you suspect that a note is counterfeit, you should report it to the nearest Nepal Rastra Bank office or police office.~

column2: ~Can I exchange any new (fresh) notes at the bank?~
column3: ~Only new (fresh) notes can be exchanged as specified by the bank. The bank can provide exchange facilities by providing any type of dry or (fresh) note of the same rate or another rate.~

column2: ~How can I get a replacement for banknotes damaged in a fire or natural disaster?~
column3: ~To get a replacement for banknotes damaged in a fire or natural disaster, an application must be submitted to the concerned police office or the concerned local body or an authorized officer as per prevailing law, along with a certified copy and a bond.~

column2: ~What can I do if I am not satisfied with the reason for the refusal to exchange the note?~
column3: ~An appeal can be filed with the appeal hearing officer within thirty-five days of receiving the notification stating the reason for the refusal to exchange the note.~

column2: ~Do the notes that the bank cannot exchange get returned to the person concerned?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, it says: "169. Disposition of Unexchangeable Banknotes: They don't. The bank refuses to exchange such notes and confiscates them and provides information along with the reason to the person concerned." Therefore, no, the notes that the bank cannot exchange do not get returned to the person concerned.~

column2: ~What kind of notes will the bank not exchange if presented for claim?~
column3: ~According to the given context, the bank will not exchange notes that have:

column1: * Mismatched numbers
* Been corrected
* Arbitrary writing
* The denomination and full number cannot be determined
* Less than 50% of the original value~

column2: ~What should be done to exchange a counterfeit note?~
column3: ~To exchange a counterfeit note, the counterfeit note must be presented to the bank. If the counterfeit note is very dirty, torn, or not whole, then for exchange, a piece of paper of the same denomination and a whole note of the same denomination must be pasted on the back of the counterfeit note and presented to the bank that exchanges the counterfeit note.~

column2: ~Where can I exchange counterfeit notes?~
column3: ~You can exchange counterfeit notes at:

column1: 1. Nepal Rastra Bank
2. The Currency Management Department
3. All provincial offices of Nepal Rastra Bank
4. All commercial banks that operate currency exchanges~

column2: ~What is meant by a tattered note?~
column3: ~A tattered note refers to a note that is torn, wrinkled, worn, burnt, wrinkled, eaten by insects, soaked in water and the printing has faded or is blurred, stained, etc.~

column2: ~On which days and at what times are the services provided to the general public by the Department of Monetary Management provided?,?~
column3: ~I don’t know based on the given context. The provided text does not mention the specific days or times when the services are provided to the general public by the Department of Monetary Management. It only provides information about various departments and their functions within Nepal Rastra Bank, but it does not specify the operating hours or schedule for each department.~

column2: ~What facilities can the general public avail from the Currency Management Department?~
column3: ~The general public can avail of the following facilities from the Currency Management Department:

column1: * Replacing uncirculated notes
* Exchanging notes
* Purchasing souvenir coins, silver coins, medallions, and gold bullion.~

column2: ~What does the Currency Management Department do?~
column3: ~The Currency Management Department designs, prints, mints/gets coins minted, and performs all necessary work for the smooth supply of currency nationwide, as well as exchanging currency and destroying unfit notes. The general public can also avail facilities such as replacing uncirculated notes, exchanging notes, purchasing souvenir coins, silver coins, medallions, and gold bullion from this department.~

column2: ~How many denominations of notes are currently in circulation in Nepal?~
column3: ~According to the given context, there are currently 11 denominations of notes in circulation in Nepal, namely Rs. 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 25, 50, 100, 250, 500 and 1000. However, notes of Rs. 1, 2, 25 and 250 have not been issued at present.~

column2: ~What is meant by Reverse Repo?~
column3: ~'Reverse Repo' is a tool used by Nepal Rastra Bank to mop up liquidity through open market transactions against the security of government bonds it owns, in the event of excess liquidity in the banking system.~

column2: ~What is an interest rate corridor?~
column3: ~The interest rate corridor is a method adopted by Nepal Rastra Bank to keep short-term interest rates, i.e. interbank interest rates, within a certain range to bring stability to long-term interest rates. It also indicates the direction of monetary policy. The rate at which the standing liquidity facility is available acts as an upper limit and the deposit collection rate acts as a lower limit. These rates are changed from time to time by monetary policy as per the need.~

column2: ~What is an interest rate corridor?~
column3: ~The lower and upper limits of the interest rates used in interbank transactions, as expected by the Nepal Rastra Bank through monetary policy, are called the interest rate corridor.~

column2: ~What is Standing Liquidity Facility (SLF)?~
column3: ~The Standing Liquidity Facility (SLF) is a credit facility provided by Nepal Rastra Bank to specified institutions against the collateral of eligible securities for short-term liquidity management. It is a tool used by Nepal Rastra Bank to provide liquidity to banks and financial institutions at their request, subject to certain conditions.~

column2: ~What is the Online Bidding System?~
column3: ~The online bidding system is an electronic medium for bidding on government bonds (Treasury Bills and Development Bonds) and monetary management instruments (repo, reverse repo, outright sale, outright purchase, deposit collection, and Nepal Rastra Bank bonds) issued through the bidding process.~

column2: ~What instruments are used to inject and absorb liquidity?~
column3: ~According to the provided context, the instruments used by Nepal Rastra Bank (NRB) to inject and absorb liquidity are:

column1: To inject liquidity:
1. Repo bids:
2. Outright purchase bids:
3. Standing Liquidity Facility (SLF): A credit facility against eligible securities for short-term liquidity management.
4. Purchase of foreign exchange (USD).
To absorb liquidity:
1. Reverse repo bids
2. Outright sale bids
3. Deposit collection bids
4. Nepal Rastra Bank bonds
Note that these instruments are mentioned in the context of open market operations and liquidity management by NRB.~

column2: ~What is the Money Market?~
column3: ~The market where instruments maturing within a year are bought and sold is called the money market. The money market provides an opportunity to make short-term investments and borrow money.~

column2: ~What is Bank Rate?~
column3: ~The interest rate charged by the central bank when providing loans against acceptable collateral to banks and financial institutions is called Bank Rate.~

column2: ~Who conducts open market transactions in Nepal?~
column3: ~The 'Open Market Transactions Operation Committee', formed under the chairmanship of the Deputy Governor of Nepal Rastra Bank, conducts open market transactions. In addition, the Monetary Management Department of the bank also conducts open market transactions.~
